FULL CONTENT:

## Contents

# Photosynthesis

![Page semi-protected](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/1b/Semi-protection-shackle.svg/20px-Semi-protection-shackle.svg.png)![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Photosynthesis_en.svg/380px-Photosynthesis_en.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Seawifs_global_biosphere.jpg/500px-Seawifs_global_biosphere.jpg)

**Photosynthesis** ([/ˌfoʊtəˈsɪnθəsɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FOH-tə-SINTH-ə-sis*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [system](/wiki/Biological_system "Biological system") of [biological processes](/wiki/Biological_process "Biological process") by which [photopigment](/wiki/Photopigment "Photopigment")-bearing [autotrophic](/wiki/Autotroph "Autotroph") [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), such as most [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae") and [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria"), convert [light energy](/wiki/Light_energy "Light energy") — typically from [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") — into the [chemical energy](/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy") necessary to fuel their [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"). The term *photosynthesis* usually refers to **oxygenic photosynthesis**, a process that releases [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") as a [byproduct](/wiki/Byproduct "Byproduct") of [water splitting](/wiki/Water_splitting "Water splitting"). Photosynthetic organisms store the converted chemical energy within the [bonds](/wiki/Chemical_bond "Chemical bond") of intracellular [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") (complex [compounds](/wiki/Chemical_compound "Chemical compound") containing [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon")), typically [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate") like [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") (mainly [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"), [fructose](/wiki/Fructose "Fructose") and [sucrose](/wiki/Sucrose "Sucrose")), [starches](/wiki/Starch "Starch"), [phytoglycogen](/wiki/Phytoglycogen "Phytoglycogen") and [cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose"). When needing to use this stored energy, an organism's cells then metabolize the organic compounds through [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"). Photosynthesis plays a critical role in producing and maintaining the [oxygen content](/wiki/Atmospheric_oxygen "Atmospheric oxygen") of the [Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere"), and it supplies most of the [biological energy](/wiki/Biological_energy "Biological energy") necessary for [complex life](/wiki/Complex_life "Complex life") on [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth").[[2]](#cite_note-Bryant-2006-2)

Some organisms also perform [anoxygenic photosynthesis](/wiki/Anoxygenic_photosynthesis "Anoxygenic photosynthesis"), which does not produce oxygen. Some [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") (e.g. [purple bacteria](/wiki/Purple_bacteria "Purple bacteria")) uses [bacteriochlorophyll](/wiki/Bacteriochlorophyll "Bacteriochlorophyll") to split [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide") as a [reductant](/wiki/Reductant "Reductant") instead of [water](/wiki/Water "Water"), releasing [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") instead of oxygen, which was a dominant form of photosynthesis in the [euxinic](/wiki/Euxinic "Euxinic") [Canfield oceans](/wiki/Canfield_ocean "Canfield ocean") during the [Boring Billion](/wiki/Boring_Billion "Boring Billion").[[3]](#cite_note-Canfield1998-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Javaux2018-4) [Archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea") such as *[Halobacterium](/wiki/Halobacterium "Halobacterium")* also perform a type of non-[carbon-fixing](/wiki/Carbon_fixation "Carbon fixation") anoxygenic photosynthesis, where the simpler photopigment [retinal](/wiki/Retinal "Retinal") and its [microbial rhodopsin](/wiki/Microbial_rhodopsin "Microbial rhodopsin") [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(chemistry) "Derivative (chemistry)") are used to absorb green light and produce a proton ([hydron](/wiki/Hydron "Hydron")) [gradient](/wiki/Electrochemical_gradient "Electrochemical gradient") across the [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane"), and the subsequent [ion movement](/wiki/Chemiosmosis "Chemiosmosis") powers [transmembrane](/wiki/Transmembrane_protein "Transmembrane protein") [proton pumps](/wiki/Proton_pump "Proton pump") to directly synthesize [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP), the "energy currency" of cells. Such archaeal photosynthesis might have been the earliest form of photosynthesis that evolved on Earth, as far back as the [Paleoarchean](/wiki/Paleoarchean "Paleoarchean"), preceding that of [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria") (see [Purple Earth hypothesis](/wiki/Purple_Earth_hypothesis "Purple Earth hypothesis")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

While the details may differ between [species](/wiki/Species "Species"), the process always begins when light energy is absorbed by the [reaction centers](/wiki/Reaction_center "Reaction center"), proteins that contain [photosynthetic pigments](/wiki/Photosynthetic_pigment "Photosynthetic pigment") or [chromophores](/wiki/Chromophore "Chromophore"). In plants, these pigments are [chlorophylls](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll") (a [porphyrin](/wiki/Porphyrin "Porphyrin") derivative that absorbs the red and blue [spectra](/wiki/Optical_spectrum "Optical spectrum") of light, thus reflecting green) held inside [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast"), abundant in [leaf](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf") cells. In cyanobacteria, they are embedded in the [plasma membrane](/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane"). In these light-dependent reactions, some energy is used to strip [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") from suitable substances, such as water, producing oxygen gas. The [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") freed by the splitting of water is used in the creation of two important molecules that participate in energetic processes: reduced [nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") (NADPH) and ATP.

In plants, [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"), and [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria"), sugars are synthesized by a subsequent sequence of light-independent reactions called the [Calvin cycle](/wiki/Calvin_cycle "Calvin cycle"). In this process, atmospheric carbon dioxide is incorporated into already existing organic compounds, such as [ribulose bisphosphate](/wiki/Ribulose_bisphosphate "Ribulose bisphosphate") (RuBP).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Using the ATP and NADPH produced by the light-dependent reactions, the resulting compounds are then [reduced](/wiki/Biological_reductant "Biological reductant") and removed to form further carbohydrates, such as [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"). In other bacteria, different mechanisms like the [reverse Krebs cycle](/wiki/Reverse_Krebs_cycle "Reverse Krebs cycle") are used to achieve the same end.

The first photosynthetic organisms probably [evolved](/wiki/Evolved "Evolved") early in the [evolutionary history of life](/wiki/Evolutionary_history_of_life "Evolutionary history of life") using [reducing agents](/wiki/Reducing_agent "Reducing agent") such as hydrogen or hydrogen sulfide, rather than water, as sources of electrons.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Cyanobacteria appeared later; the [excess oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen_cycle "Oxygen cycle") they produced contributed directly to the [oxygenation of the Earth](/wiki/Oxygenation_of_the_Earth "Oxygenation of the Earth"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) which rendered the evolution of complex life possible. The average rate of energy captured by global photosynthesis is approximately 130 [terawatts](/wiki/Terawatts "Terawatts"),[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) which is about eight times the total [power consumption of human civilization](/wiki/Power_consumption_of_human_civilization "Power consumption of human civilization").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Photosynthetic organisms also convert around 100–115 billion [tons](/wiki/Ton "Ton") (91–104 Pg [petagrams](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(mass) "Orders of magnitude (mass)"), or billions of metric tons), of carbon into [biomass](/wiki/Biomass_(ecology) "Biomass (ecology)") per year.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-McGraw-Hill-2007-14) Photosynthesis was discovered in 1779 by [Jan Ingenhousz](/wiki/Jan_Ingenhousz "Jan Ingenhousz") who showed that plants need light, not just soil and water.

## Overview

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Simple_photosynthesis_overview.svg/250px-Simple_photosynthesis_overview.svg.png)

Most photosynthetic organisms are [photoautotrophs](/wiki/Photoautotroph "Photoautotroph"), which means that they are able to [synthesize](/wiki/Chemical_synthesis "Chemical synthesis") food directly from [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") and [water](/wiki/Water "Water") using [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") from light. However, not all organisms use carbon dioxide as a source of carbon atoms to carry out photosynthesis; [photoheterotrophs](/wiki/Photoheterotroph "Photoheterotroph") use organic compounds, rather than carbon dioxide, as a source of carbon.[[2]](#cite_note-Bryant-2006-2)

In [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"), and [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria"), photosynthesis releases oxygen. This **oxygenic photosynthesis** is by far the most common type of photosynthesis used by living organisms. Some shade-loving plants (sciophytes) produce such low levels of oxygen during photosynthesis that they use all of it themselves instead of releasing it to the atmosphere.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Although there are some differences between oxygenic photosynthesis in plants, algae, and cyanobacteria, the overall process is quite similar in these organisms. There are also many varieties of [anoxygenic photosynthesis](/wiki/Anoxygenic_photosynthesis "Anoxygenic photosynthesis"), used mostly by bacteria, which consume carbon dioxide but do not release oxygen or which produce elemental sulfur instead of molecular oxygen.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Carbon dioxide is converted into sugars in a process called [carbon fixation](/wiki/Carbon_fixation "Carbon fixation"); photosynthesis captures energy from sunlight to convert carbon dioxide into [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate"). Carbon fixation is an [endothermic](/wiki/Endothermic "Endothermic") [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox") reaction. In general outline, photosynthesis is the opposite of [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"): while photosynthesis is a process of reduction of carbon dioxide to carbohydrates, cellular respiration is the oxidation of carbohydrates or other [nutrients](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient") to carbon dioxide. Nutrients used in cellular respiration include carbohydrates, amino acids and fatty acids. These nutrients are oxidized to produce carbon dioxide and water, and to release chemical energy to drive the organism's [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism").

Photosynthesis and cellular respiration are distinct processes, as they take place through different sequences of chemical reactions and in different [cellular compartments](/wiki/Cellular_compartment "Cellular compartment") (cellular respiration in [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria")).[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The general [equation](/wiki/Chemical_equation "Chemical equation") for photosynthesis as first proposed by [Cornelis van Niel](/wiki/C._B._van_Niel "C. B. van Niel") is:[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmarshGovindjee199913-20)

Since water is used as the electron donor in oxygenic photosynthesis, the equation for this process is:

This equation emphasizes that water is both a reactant in the [light-dependent reaction](#Light-dependent_reactions) and a product of the [light-independent reaction](#Light-independent_reactions), but canceling *n* water molecules from each side gives the net equation:

Other processes substitute other compounds (such as [arsenite](/wiki/Arsenite "Arsenite")) for water in the electron-supply role; for example some microbes use sunlight to oxidize arsenite to [arsenate](/wiki/Arsenate "Arsenate"):[[21]](#cite_note-21) The equation for this reaction is:

Photosynthesis occurs in two stages. In the first stage, *light-dependent reactions* or *light reactions* capture the energy of light and use it to make the hydrogen carrier [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH") and the energy-storage molecule [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate"). During the second stage, the *light-independent reactions* use these products to capture and reduce carbon dioxide.

Most organisms that use oxygenic photosynthesis use [visible light](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") for the light-dependent reactions, although at least three use shortwave [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") or, more specifically, far-red radiation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Some organisms employ even more radical variants of photosynthesis. Some [archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea") use a simpler method that employs a pigment similar to those used for vision in animals. The [bacteriorhodopsin](/wiki/Bacteriorhodopsin "Bacteriorhodopsin") changes its configuration in response to sunlight, acting as a proton pump. This produces a proton gradient more directly, which is then converted to chemical energy. The process does not involve carbon dioxide fixation and does not release oxygen, and seems to have evolved separately from the more common types of photosynthesis.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Photosynthetic membranes and organelles

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Chloroplast.svg/500px-Chloroplast.svg.png)

In photosynthetic bacteria, the proteins that gather light for photosynthesis are embedded in [cell membranes](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane"). In its simplest form, this involves the membrane surrounding the cell itself.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, the membrane may be tightly folded into cylindrical sheets called [thylakoids](/wiki/Thylakoid "Thylakoid"),[[26]](#cite_note-Mullineaux-1999-26) or bunched up into round [vesicles](/wiki/Vesicle_(biology) "Vesicle (biology)") called *intracytoplasmic membranes*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) These structures can fill most of the interior of a cell, giving the membrane a very large surface area and therefore increasing the amount of light that the bacteria can absorb.[[26]](#cite_note-Mullineaux-1999-26)

In plants and algae, photosynthesis takes place in [organelles](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") called [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast"). A typical [plant cell](/wiki/Plant_cell "Plant cell") contains about 10 to 100 chloroplasts. The chloroplast is enclosed by a membrane. This membrane is composed of a phospholipid inner membrane, a phospholipid outer membrane, and an intermembrane space. Enclosed by the membrane is an aqueous fluid called the stroma. Embedded within the stroma are stacks of thylakoids (grana), which are the site of photosynthesis. The thylakoids appear as flattened disks. The thylakoid itself is enclosed by the thylakoid membrane, and within the enclosed volume is a lumen or thylakoid space. Embedded in the thylakoid membrane are integral and [peripheral membrane protein](/wiki/Peripheral_membrane_protein "Peripheral membrane protein") complexes of the photosynthetic system.

Plants absorb light primarily using the [pigment](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment") [chlorophyll](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll"). The green part of the light spectrum is not absorbed but is reflected, which is the reason that most plants have a green color. Besides chlorophyll, plants also use pigments such as [carotenes](/wiki/Carotene "Carotene") and [xanthophylls](/wiki/Xanthophyll "Xanthophyll").[[28]](#cite_note-28) Algae also use chlorophyll, but various other pigments are present, such as [phycocyanin](/wiki/Phycocyanin "Phycocyanin"), [carotenes](/wiki/Carotene "Carotene"), and [xanthophylls](/wiki/Xanthophyll "Xanthophyll") in [green algae](/wiki/Green_algae "Green algae"), [phycoerythrin](/wiki/Phycoerythrin "Phycoerythrin") in [red algae](/wiki/Red_algae "Red algae") (rhodophytes) and [fucoxanthin](/wiki/Fucoxanthin "Fucoxanthin") in [brown algae](/wiki/Brown_algae "Brown algae") and [diatoms](/wiki/Diatoms "Diatoms") resulting in a wide variety of colors.

These pigments are embedded in plants and algae in complexes called antenna proteins. In such proteins, the pigments are arranged to work together. Such a combination of proteins is also called a [light-harvesting complex](/wiki/Light-harvesting_complex "Light-harvesting complex").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Although all cells in the green parts of a plant have chloroplasts, the majority of those are found in specially adapted structures called [leaves](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf"). Certain species adapted to conditions of strong sunlight and [aridity](/wiki/Arid "Arid"), such as many *[Euphorbia](/wiki/Euphorbia "Euphorbia")* and [cactus](/wiki/Cactus "Cactus") species, have their main photosynthetic organs in their stems. The cells in the interior tissues of a leaf, called the [mesophyll](/wiki/Mesophyll_tissue "Mesophyll tissue"), can contain between 450,000 and 800,000 chloroplasts for every square millimeter of leaf. The surface of the leaf is coated with a water-resistant [waxy](/wiki/Wax "Wax") [cuticle](/wiki/Plant_cuticle "Plant cuticle") that protects the leaf from excessive [evaporation](/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation") of water and decreases the absorption of [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") or [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue") [light](/wiki/Light "Light") to minimize [heating](/wiki/Heat "Heat"). The transparent [epidermis](/wiki/Leaf#Epidermis "Leaf") layer allows light to pass through to the [palisade](/wiki/Mesophyll_tissue "Mesophyll tissue") mesophyll cells where most of the photosynthesis takes place.

## Light-dependent reactions

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Thylakoid_membrane_3.svg/500px-Thylakoid_membrane_3.svg.png)

In the [light-dependent reactions](/wiki/Light-dependent_reactions "Light-dependent reactions"), one [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") of the pigment [chlorophyll](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll") absorbs one [photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon") and loses one [electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron"). This electron is taken up by a modified form of chlorophyll called [pheophytin](/wiki/Pheophytin "Pheophytin"), which passes the electron to a [quinone](/wiki/Quinone "Quinone") molecule, starting the flow of electrons down an [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain") that leads to the ultimate [reduction](/wiki/Redox "Redox") of [NADP](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") to [NADPH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate"). In addition, this creates a [proton gradient](/wiki/Electrochemical_gradient "Electrochemical gradient") (energy gradient) across the [chloroplast membrane](/wiki/Chloroplast_membrane "Chloroplast membrane"), which is used by [ATP synthase](/wiki/ATP_synthase "ATP synthase") in the synthesis of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate"). The chlorophyll molecule ultimately regains the electron it lost when a [water](/wiki/Water "Water") molecule is split in a process called [photolysis](/wiki/Photodissociation "Photodissociation"), which releases [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen").

The overall equation for the light-dependent reactions under the conditions of non-cyclic electron flow in green plants is:[[30]](#cite_note-Raven-2005-30)

Not all [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") of [light](/wiki/Light "Light") can support photosynthesis. The photosynthetic [action spectrum](/wiki/Action_spectrum "Action spectrum") depends on the type of [accessory pigments](/wiki/Accessory_pigment "Accessory pigment") present. For example, in [green plants](/wiki/Green_plants "Green plants"), the action spectrum resembles the [absorption spectrum](/wiki/Absorption_spectrum "Absorption spectrum") for [chlorophylls](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll") and [carotenoids](/wiki/Carotenoid "Carotenoid") with absorption peaks in violet-blue and red light. In [red algae](/wiki/Red_algae "Red algae"), the action spectrum is blue-green light, which allows these [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae") to use the blue end of the spectrum to grow in the deeper waters that filter out the longer wavelengths (red light) used by above-ground green plants. The non-absorbed part of the [light spectrum](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum") is what gives [photosynthetic organisms](/wiki/Photosynthetic_organism "Photosynthetic organism") their [color](/wiki/Color "Color") (e.g., green plants, red algae, [purple bacteria](/wiki/Purple_bacteria "Purple bacteria")) and is the least effective for photosynthesis in the respective [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism").

### Z scheme

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Z-scheme.png/750px-Z-scheme.png)

In [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), [light-dependent reactions](/wiki/Light-dependent_reaction "Light-dependent reaction") occur in the [thylakoid membranes](/wiki/Thylakoid_membrane "Thylakoid membrane") of the [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast") where they drive the synthesis of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") and [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH"). The light-dependent reactions are of two forms: [cyclic and non-cyclic](/wiki/Photophosphorylation "Photophosphorylation").

In the non-cyclic reaction, the photons are captured in the light-harvesting [antenna complexes](/wiki/Antenna_complex "Antenna complex") of [photosystem II](/wiki/Photosystem "Photosystem") by [chlorophyll](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll") and other [accessory pigments](/wiki/Accessory_pigments "Accessory pigments") (see diagram "Z-scheme"). The absorption of a photon by the antenna complex loosens an electron by a process called [photoinduced charge separation](/wiki/Photoinduced_charge_separation "Photoinduced charge separation"). The antenna system is at the core of the [chlorophyll](/wiki/Chlorophyll "Chlorophyll") molecule of the photosystem II reaction center. That loosened electron is taken up by the primary [electron-acceptor](/wiki/Electron_acceptor "Electron acceptor") molecule, [pheophytin](/wiki/Pheophytin "Pheophytin"). As the electrons are shuttled through an [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transfer_chain "Electron transfer chain") (the so-called *Z-scheme* shown in the diagram), a [chemiosmotic potential](/wiki/Chemiosmotic_potential "Chemiosmotic potential") is generated by pumping [proton cations](/wiki/Hydron_(chemistry) "Hydron (chemistry)") ([H](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen")+) across the [membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") and into the [thylakoid space](/wiki/Thylakoid_space "Thylakoid space"). An ATP synthase [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") uses that [chemiosmotic potential](/wiki/Chemiosmotic_potential "Chemiosmotic potential") to make ATP during [photophosphorylation](/wiki/Photophosphorylation "Photophosphorylation"), whereas [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH") is a product of the terminal [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox") reaction in the *Z-scheme*. The electron enters a chlorophyll [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") in [Photosystem I](/wiki/Photosystem_I "Photosystem I"). There it is further excited by the [light](/wiki/Light "Light") absorbed by that [photosystem](/wiki/Photosystem "Photosystem"). The electron is then passed along a chain of [electron acceptors](/wiki/Electron_acceptor "Electron acceptor") to which it transfers some of its [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"). The energy delivered to the electron acceptors is used to move [hydrogen ions](/wiki/Hydrogen_ion "Hydrogen ion") across the thylakoid membrane into the [lumen](/wiki/Lumen_(anatomy) "Lumen (anatomy)"). The electron is eventually used to [reduce](/wiki/Redox "Redox") the coenzyme [NADP](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") with an [H+](/wiki/Hydron_(chemistry) "Hydron (chemistry)") to NADPH (which has functions in the light-independent reaction); at that point, the path of that electron ends.

The cyclic reaction is similar to that of the non-cyclic but differs in that it generates only ATP, and no reduced NADP (NADPH) is created. The cyclic reaction takes place only at photosystem I. Once the electron is displaced from the photosystem, the electron is passed down the electron acceptor molecules and returns to photosystem I, from where it was emitted, hence the name *cyclic reaction*.

### Water photolysis

[Linear electron transport](#Z_scheme) through a photosystem will leave the [reaction center](/wiki/Photosynthetic_reaction_centre "Photosynthetic reaction centre") of that photosystem [oxidized](/wiki/Oxidized "Oxidized"). Elevating another electron will first require re-reduction of the reaction center. The excited electrons lost from the reaction center ([P700](/wiki/P700 "P700")) of [photosystem I](/wiki/Photosystem_I "Photosystem I") are replaced by transfer from [plastocyanin](/wiki/Plastocyanin "Plastocyanin"), whose electrons come from electron transport through [photosystem II](/wiki/Photosystem_II "Photosystem II"). Photosystem II, as the first step of the *Z-scheme*, requires an external source of electrons to reduce its oxidized [chlorophyll *a*](/wiki/Chlorophyll_a "Chlorophyll a") reaction center. The source of electrons for photosynthesis in green plants and [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria") is water. Two water molecules are oxidized by the energy of four successive charge-separation reactions of photosystem II to yield a molecule of [diatomic](/wiki/Diatomic_molecule "Diatomic molecule") oxygen and four [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") ions. The electrons yielded are transferred to a redox-active [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine") residue that is oxidized by the energy of [P680+](/wiki/P680 "P680"). This resets the ability of P680 to absorb another photon and release another [photo-dissociated](/wiki/Photodissociation "Photodissociation") electron. The oxidation of water is [catalyzed](/wiki/Catalysis "Catalysis") in photosystem II by a redox-active structure that contains four [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese") ions and a [calcium ion](/wiki/Calcium_ion "Calcium ion"); this [oxygen-evolving complex](/wiki/Oxygen-evolving_complex "Oxygen-evolving complex") binds two [water molecules](/wiki/Water_molecules "Water molecules") and contains the four oxidizing equivalents that are used to drive the water-oxidizing reaction (Kok's S-state diagrams). The hydrogen ions are released in the [thylakoid lumen](/wiki/Thylakoid#Lumen "Thylakoid") and therefore contribute to the transmembrane chemiosmotic potential that leads to [ATP synthesis](/wiki/ATP_synthesis "ATP synthesis"). Oxygen is a [waste product](/wiki/By-product "By-product") of light-dependent reactions, but the majority of organisms on [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") use oxygen and its energy for [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"), including [photosynthetic organisms](/wiki/Photosynthetic_organism "Photosynthetic organism").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Light-independent reactions

### Calvin cycle

In the [light-independent](/wiki/Light-independent_reactions "Light-independent reactions") (or "dark") reactions, the enzyme [RuBisCO](/wiki/RuBisCO "RuBisCO") captures [CO2](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") from the [atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere") and, in a [process](/wiki/Biological_process "Biological process") called the [Calvin cycle](/wiki/Calvin_cycle "Calvin cycle"), uses the newly formed [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH") and releases [three-carbon sugars](/wiki/Triose "Triose"), which are later [combined](/wiki/Condensation_reaction "Condensation reaction") to form [sucrose](/wiki/Sucrose "Sucrose") and [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch"). The overall equation for the light-independent reactions in [green plants](/wiki/Green_plants "Green plants") is[[30]](#cite_note-Raven-2005-30): 128

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Calvin-cycle4.svg/500px-Calvin-cycle4.svg.png)

[Carbon fixation](/wiki/Carbon_fixation "Carbon fixation") produces the [three-carbon sugar intermediate](/wiki/Glyceraldehyde_3-phosphate "Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate"), which is then converted into the final [carbohydrate](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate") products. The [simple carbon sugars](/wiki/Monosaccharide "Monosaccharide") photosynthesis produces are then used to form other [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound"), such as the building material [cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose"), the [precursors](/wiki/Precursor_(chemistry) "Precursor (chemistry)") for [lipid](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid") and [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") biosynthesis, or as a fuel in [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"). The latter occurs not only in [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant") but also in [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal") when the [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") from plants is passed through a [food chain](/wiki/Food_chain "Food chain").

# What is the photosynthesis equation?

![Flexi Avatar](/flexi/flexi-full-circle.svg)

## Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. The equation for photosynthesis can be written as: @$$\begin{align\*} 6CO\_2 + 6H\_2O + \text{light energy} \rightarrow C\_6H\_{12}O\_6 + 6O\_2 \end{align\*}@$$ Here's what each part represents: * @$\begin{align\*}6CO\_2\end{align\*}@$: Six molecules of carbon dioxide * @$\begin{align\*}6H\_2O\end{align\*}@$: Six molecules of water * @$\begin{align\*}\text{light energy}\end{align\*}@$: Energy from sunlight * @$\begin{align\*}C\_6H\_{12}O\_6\end{align\*}@$: One molecule of glucose (a sugar) * @$\begin{align\*}6O\_2\end{align\*}@$: Six molecules of oxygen This process takes place in the chloroplasts of plant cells, where chlorophyll captures the light energy needed to drive the reaction. It's like nature's way of making food and oxygen! ð¿ð

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. The equation for photosynthesis can be written as:

@$$\begin{align\*} 6CO\_2 + 6H\_2O + \text{light energy} \rightarrow C\_6H\_{12}O\_6 + 6O\_2 \end{align\*}@$$

Here's what each part represents:

This process takes place in the chloroplasts of plant cells, where chlorophyll captures the light energy needed to drive the reaction. It's like nature's way of making food and oxygen! ð¿ð

# 4.1.3: Photosynthesis Overview and Equation

![ ](https://biz.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/5084/girl-160172__340.png "Melissa Ha, Maria Morrow, & Kammy Algiers")

##### Learning Objectives

Plants, algae, and certain bacteria (cyanobacteria and green and purple sulfur bacteria) are among the organisms capable of performing photosynthesis (Figure \(\PageIndex{1}\)). Because they use light to manufacture their own food, they are called **photoautotrophs** (literally, “self-feeders using light”). Other organisms, such as animals, fungi, and most other bacteria, are termed **heterotrophs** (“other feeders”), because they must rely on the sugars produced by photosynthetic organisms for their energy needs. A third very interesting group of bacteria synthesize sugars, not by using sunlight’s energy, but by extracting energy from inorganic chemical compounds; hence, they are referred to as **chemoautotrophs**.

![A fern leaf, green algae growing on water, cyanobacteria, black smoke pouring out of a deep sea vent covered with red worms, and rod-shaped bacteria.](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/1085/Figure_08_01_01.jpg?revision=1)

The importance of photosynthesis is not just that it can capture sunlight’s energy. Photosynthesis is vital because it evolved as a way to store the energy in solar radiation (the “photo-” part) as high-energy electrons in the carbon-carbon bonds of carbohydrate molecules (the “-synthesis” part). Those carbohydrates are the energy source that heterotrophs use to power the synthesis of ATP via celluar respiration. Therefore, photosynthesis powers 99 percent of Earth’s ecosystems.

## Photosynthesis Equation

Photosynthesis is a multi-step process that requires sunlight, carbon dioxide (which is low in energy), and water as substrates (Figure \(\PageIndex{2}\)). After the process is complete, it releases oxygen and produces glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (GA3P), simple carbohydrate molecules (which are high in energy) that can subsequently be converted into glucose, sucrose, or any of dozens of other sugar molecules. These sugar molecules contain energy and the energized carbon that all living things need to survive.

![Photo of a tree. Arrows indicate that the tree uses carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight to make sugars and oxygen.](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/1087/Figure_08_01_03.jpg?revision=1)

The following is the chemical equation for photosynthesis (Figure \(\PageIndex{3}\)). Although the equation looks simple, the many steps that take place during photosynthesis are actually quite complex.

![In the photosynthesis equation, six carbon dioxide and six water molecules produce one sugar molecule and six oxygen molecules.](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/1088/Figure_08_01_04.jpg?revision=1)

## Structures of Photosynthesis

Before learning the details of how photoautotrophs turn sunlight into food, it is important to review the structures involved. Recall from the [Leaves chapter](https://bio.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Botany/Botany_(Ha_Morrow_and_Algiers)/03%3A_Plant_Structure/3.04%3A_Leaves "12: Leaves") that leaves consist of several layers of cells, and the process of photosynthesis occurs in the **mesophyll** (middle layer). Note that other parts of the plant, such as the stems, are photosynthetic as well. The gas exchange of carbon dioxide and oxygen occurs through the **stomata** (singular: stoma), which also participate in water balance. The stomata are typically located on the underside of the leaf, which helps to minimize water loss. Each stoma is flanked by **guard cells** that regulate their opening and closing.

In all autotrophic eukaryotes, photosynthesis takes place inside the **chloroplast**. For plants, chloroplast-containing cells exist in the mesophyll. Chloroplasts have a double membrane envelope (composed of an outer membrane and an inner membrane). Within the chloroplast are stacked, disc-shaped structures called **thylakoids**. Embedded in the thylakoid membrane is **chlorophyll**, a **pigment** (molecule that absorbs light) responsible for the initial interaction between light and plant material, and numerous proteins that make up the **electron transport chain**. The thylakoid membrane encloses an internal space called the **thylakoid lumen**. As shown in Figure \(\PageIndex{4}\), a stack of thylakoids is called a **granum** (plural: grana), and the liquid-filled space surrounding the granum is called **stroma** or “bed” (not to be confused with stoma or “mouth,” an opening on the leaf epidermis).

![A chloroplast, which has an outer membrane and an inner membrane](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/1089/Figure_08_01_05.png?revision=1)

## Steps of Photosynthesis

Photosynthesis takes place in two sequential stages: the **light-dependent reactions** and the **light independent-reactions** (Calvin cycle). In the light-dependent reactions, energy from sunlight is absorbed by chlorophyll and that energy is converted into stored chemical energy. Light-dependent reactions require water and produce oxygen and energy in the form of ATP. In the light-independent reactions, the chemical energy harvested during the light-dependent reactions drive the assembly of sugar molecules from carbon dioxide. Therefore, although the light-independent reactions do not use light as a reactant, they require the products of the light-dependent reactions to function. In addition, several enzymes of the light-independent reactions are activated by light.

The light-dependent reactions utilize ATP and **nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate** (**NADPH**) to temporarily store the energy. Because NADPH carries two high-energy electrons, it is often referred to as an **electron carrier**. It can be thought of as “full” because it is rich in energy in its reduced state. When a molecule is reduced, electrons have been added to it. Electrons have a negative charge, so this is termed “**reduction”**. NAPDH move energy from light-dependent reactions to light-independent reactions. After the energy is released, the “empty” (**oxidized**) version, NADP+, returns to the light-dependent reactions to obtain more high-energy electrons. Likewise, the ATP produced during the light-dependent reactions fuels the light-independent reactions. It then returns in a lower-energy form (ADP) to the light-dependent reactions to become phosphorylated again. Figure \(\PageIndex{5}\) illustrates the components inside the chloroplast where the light-dependent and light-independent reactions take place.

![A chloroplast shows the light-dependent reactions in the thylakoid membranes and the Calvin cycle in the stroma.](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/1090/Figure_08_01_06.jpg?revision=1)

enriched_points.expanded_content: The overall equation is 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy → C6H12O6 + 6O2, demonstrating how algae convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen.
Chlorophyll is a key pigment in algae, absorbing light energy primarily from the blue and red wavelengths, which drives the photosynthesis process.
Algae utilize the Calvin cycle for carbon fixation, converting CO2 into organic compounds, which are essential for energy storage and biomass production.
